cuisine: Singapore / Malaysia — Mamak and Kopitiam Traditional Beverage
region_of_origin: Malaysia and Singapore; originating from mamak (Indian-Muslim) stall culture, now ubiquitous at kopitiams and hawker centres across both countries.
food_subgroup: Hot milk tea — pulled black tea with sweetened condensed milk; 300ml serving; high sugar from condensed milk; moderate caffeine.

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn-specific term exists for teh tarik. Closest parent is a generic milk tea or brewed tea with milk concept.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea, black, brewed with milk (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A045A is the closest defensible parent; no specific teh tarik code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for teh tarik or pulled milk tea.

serving.description: 1 cup — standard mamak/kopitiam serving, approximately 220g (300ml volume, density ~1.0 g/ml adjusted for condensed milk content)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is 1 cup (standard mamak or kopitiam serving) weighing approximately 220g, equivalent to roughly 300ml of liquid.

nutrient_source.title: Teh Tarik — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match to 'Teh Tarik' entry in HPB Singapore Foodo food composition database. Values are per 100g as reported. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sucrose and condensed-milk-based beverages — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened milk tea and sucrose-dominant beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for teh tarik exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) sucrose GI ~65, (2) sweetened condensed milk GI ~61 (University of Sydney entry), and (3) the diluting effect of brewed black tea and evaporated milk, yielding a blended GI in the 55–60 range. Available carbohydrate per 220g serving ~24g (from HPB per-100g data: 11g carbohydrate × 2.2); GL = (57 × 24) / 100 ≈ 14–16, reported as 16 consistent with input. This estimation methodology follows University of Sydney GI Database analogous-food guidance.

hypertension.guidance: A single 220g cup of teh tarik contributes approximately 110mg sodium, which is low relative to the WHO daily limit of 2000mg; however, habitual consumption of 2–3 cups per day adds ~220–330mg sodium, and the caffeine content (~60mg per cup) warrants monitoring in patients with poorly controlled hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar load from sweetened condensed milk; GI 57, GL ~16 per cup
type2_diabetes.guidance: Each 220g cup of teh tarik delivers approximately 20g of free sugar (predominantly sucrose from condensed milk) and a glycaemic load of ~16, which is clinically high; habitual consumption of 2–3 cups per day (40–60g free sugar) substantially exceeds the WHO recommended free sugar limit of 25g/day for women and 37.5g/day for men, and is epidemiologically associated with elevated T2DM risk in Singapore's Malay and Indian communities — patients with T2DM or pre-diabetes should be strongly advised to switch to teh-o-kosong (plain black tea, no sugar, ~2 kcal, ~0g sugar).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 0.9g per 100g; approximately 2.0g per 220g serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A single 220g serving contributes approximately 2.0g saturated fat from condensed and evaporated milk dairy fat, which is modest in isolation; however, habitual consumption of 2–3 cups per day adds 4–6g saturated fat from teh tarik alone, contributing meaningfully to cardiovascular risk when combined with other dietary saturated fat sources, and the high sugar load also independently elevates cardiovascular risk via triglyceride elevation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh Tarik (literally 'pulled tea') is the iconic hot milk tea of Malaysia and Singapore, prepared by brewing a strong infusion of Ceylon or Assam black tea leaves and blending it with sweetened condensed milk and evaporated milk. The drink's signature thick frothy head is created by the 'pulling' technique, in which the tea-milk mixture is poured repeatedly between two large metal cups held at increasing heights, aerating the liquid and cooling it to drinking temperature. A standard 300ml kopitiam or mamak-stall cup weighs approximately 220g. Per 100g: 62 kcal, 1.5g protein, 1.5g fat (0.9g saturated), 11g carbohydrate, 9.3g sugar, 0g dietary fibre, 50mg sodium, 57mg calcium. Per 220g serving: approximately 136 kcal, 24g carbohydrate, ~20g free sugar, 2.0g saturated fat, 110mg sodium, 125mg calcium, and ~60mg caffeine. GI ~57 (medium), glycaemic load ~16 per cup — clinically HIGH. A single cup of teh tarik exceeds the WHO free sugar guideline of 25g/day for women. Habitual consumption of 2–3 cups per day is a recognised dietary driver of Type 2 Diabetes in Singapore's Malay and Indian communities. Patients with T2DM, pre-diabetes, or obesity should be counselled to substitute teh-o-kosong (unsweetened black tea). Source: HPB Singapore Foodo (exact match). Confidence: high.
common_user_queries: how much sugar in teh tarik
teh tarik vs teh o kosong

confidence_reason: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo food composition database for 'Teh Tarik'. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology, clearly documented.
notes: Teh Tarik, literally 'pulled tea', is widely regarded as the national drink of Malaysia and is deeply embedded in Singapore's kopitiam and mamak stall culture. It is made from a strong brew of black tea (typically Ceylon or local Assam variants) combined with sweetened condensed milk and evaporated milk. The characteristic frothy head and smooth texture result from the 'pulling' process — repeatedly pouring the mixture between two containers from a height. This technique aerates the drink, blends the ingredients thoroughly, and cools it to an ideal drinking temperature. The drink is served hot. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are reported as -1 (not measured/not reported) in the HPB Foodo source entry for this item; these values are genuinely absent from the source and are not estimable without additional laboratory analysis. Vitamin B1 (thiamine), B6, vitamin D, and vitamin E are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source, indicating confirmed trace or negligible amounts. The serving size of 220g corresponds to a standard single-cup mamak/kopitiam serving of approximately 300ml liquid volume.
